The **D Sixth Avenue Express**[[3]](#cite_note-timetable-3) is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") service in the [B Division](/wiki/B_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "B Division (New York City Subway)") of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"). Its route emblem, or "bullet", is colored orange, since it uses the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") as its main trunk line in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The D operates 24 hours daily between [205th Street](/wiki/Norwood-205th_Street_(IND_Concourse_Line) "Norwood-205th Street (IND Concourse Line)") in [Norwood, Bronx](/wiki/Norwood,_Bronx "Norwood, Bronx"), and [Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_(New_York_City_Subway) "Coney Island-Stillwell Avenue (New York City Subway)") in [Coney Island, Brooklyn](/wiki/Coney_Island,_Brooklyn "Coney Island, Brooklyn"). Daytime service makes express stops in Manhattan, and in Brooklyn (between [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [36th Street](/wiki/36th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "36th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)")) and also bypasses [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Lines) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Lines)"). During rush hours in the peak direction, D service also makes express stops between [Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road_(IND_Concourse_Line) "Fordham Road (IND Concourse Line)") in the Bronx and [145th Street](/wiki/145th_Street_(IND_Lines) "145th Street (IND Lines)") in Manhattan. Overnight service is the same as daytime service, except all trains make all stops in Brooklyn and stops at DeKalb Avenue.

In its early years, the D ran to Chambers Street/Hudson Terminal in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") via the lower [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") south of West 4th Street. From 1954 to 1967, the D ran via the [IND Culver Line](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line") to Coney Island. With the completion of the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection"), service was rerouted via the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line"), running there from 1967 to 2001. As part of the multi-year rebuilding of the Manhattan Bridge, a short-lived yellow D service ran via the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in Manhattan to the Brighton Line in Brooklyn, while orange D service used the Sixth Avenue, [Central Park West](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line"), and Concourse Lines in Manhattan and the Bronx.

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=D_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/1962_water_main_break_service_changes.gif/250px-1962_water_main_break_service_changes.gif)](/wiki/File:1962_water_main_break_service_changes.gif)

A poster showing the temporary DD service that resulted from a water main break

D service began on December 15, 1940, when the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") opened. It ran from 205th Street, the Bronx to World Trade Center (at that time called Hudson Terminal) on the [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") at all times, switching between the IND Sixth Avenue to the Eighth Avenue Lines just south of West Fourth Street–Washington Square.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Service ran express via the Concourse Line during rush hours.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Two trains started service at Bedford Park Boulevard in the morning rush hour.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

D service was increased on October 24, 1949, in order to offset the discontinuation of [C](/wiki/CC_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "CC (New York City Subway service)") service, which ran express via the Concourse Line and the Eighth Avenue Line.[[8]](#cite_note-8) After the morning rush hour on weekdays, several D trains terminated at Bedford Park Boulevard. On December 29, 1951, Saturday peak direction express service in the Bronx was discontinued, along with the discontinuation of Saturday CC local service.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

On October 30, 1954, the Culver Ramp opened, providing a connection between the [IND South Brooklyn Line](/wiki/IND_South_Brooklyn_Line "IND South Brooklyn Line") and [BMT Culver Line](/wiki/BMT_Culver_Line "BMT Culver Line"). D service was rerouted via these two lines to Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue with alternate trains running to [Church Avenue](/wiki/Church_Avenue_(IND_Culver_Line) "Church Avenue (IND Culver Line)") during rush hours.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On Saturdays, four round trips ran between 205th Street and [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway_(IND_Culver_Line) "Kings Highway (IND Culver Line)").[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11) D trains replaced F service on the South Brooklyn Line, and were sent over the new connection as the first IND service to reach Coney Island. The service was announced as Concourse–Culver and advertised as direct Bronx–Coney Island service.[[12]](#cite_note-Sparberg2014-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

On May 13, 1957, alternate D trains were cut back to Church Avenue during weekday middays.[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11) Between October 7, 1957, and 1959, four rush hour trains ran to [Euclid Avenue](/wiki/Euclid_Avenue_(IND_Fulton_Street_Line) "Euclid Avenue (IND Fulton Street Line)") via the [IND Fulton Street Line](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line") when the D started being inspected at [Pitkin Yard](/wiki/Pitkin_Yard "Pitkin Yard").[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) Four trains left 205th Street between 7:20 and 8:10 a.m., and one left Bedford Park Boulevard at 8:53 a.m. These four trains returned between 3 and 5 p.m. During the morning rush hour, several northbound trains ended at Bedford Park Boulevard.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11) These trains ran express along the Fulton Street Line if they ran during the hour that A trains ran express along the line.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

From December 4 to 27, 1962, a special service labeled DD was provided due to a water main break. It operated during rush hours only between 205th Street and Stillwell Avenue. It made all stops between 205th Street in the Bronx and [59th Street–Columbus Circle](/wiki/59th_Street%E2%80%93Columbus_Circle_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street–Columbus Circle (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") in Manhattan, rerouted via the Eighth Avenue Local between 59th and [West Fourth Streets](/wiki/West_Fourth_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "West Fourth Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)"), and then made all stops between West Fourth Street in Manhattan and Stillwell Avenue in Brooklyn via the Culver Line.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Chrystie Street Connection (1960–1981)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=D_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chrystie Street Connection (1960–1981)")]

On November 26, 1967, the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection") opened, connecting the Sixth Avenue Line with the north tracks of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") and the BMT Southern Division lines in Brooklyn. In conjunction with this project, the new express tracks on the Sixth Avenue Line between [West Fourth Street–Washington Square](/wiki/West_Fourth_Street%E2%80%93Washington_Square_(New_York_City_Subway) "West Fourth Street–Washington Square (New York City Subway)") and [34th Street](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Herald_Square_(New_York_City_Subway) "34th Street–Herald Square (New York City Subway)") were opened, providing additional capacity for the extra trains on the IND via the connection.[[15]](#cite_note-15) On this date, D service was switched over to [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line") via this new connector, running express on weekdays to [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)") and local to Stillwell Avenue at all other times. The D replaced [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") service, which had run local in Brooklyn (except during morning rush hours and early evenings) and express on the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in Manhattan, terminating at [57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_%E2%80%93_Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street – Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)"). In Manhattan, it ran express from West 4th Street to [34th Street](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Herald_Square_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "34th Street–Herald Square (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") rush hours only, with the [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)") using the express tracks to relay when it terminated at West 4th Street at other times. Service on the Culver Line to Coney Island was replaced by extended F service.[[12]](#cite_note-Sparberg2014-12)[[16]](#cite_note-NYTimes-ChrystieStChanges-1967-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) On July 1, 1968, it would become the full-time Sixth Avenue Express when non-rush hours [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)") service and new [KK](/wiki/KK_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "KK (New York City Subway service)") service was extended to the new [57th Street–Sixth Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "57th Street (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") station.[[18]](#cite_note-:12-18)

On August 19, 1968, to reduce conflicts at the Brighton Beach terminal, D service was truncated to [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)") when it ran express on the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line") (morning rush hours through early evenings, and QB (rush-hour peak direction only) and QJ (morning rush hours through early evenings) were extended from Brighton Beach to [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(New_York_City_Subway) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (New York City Subway)"). In addition, the span of Manhattan-bound D express service was increased by two hours, with the last express leaving Brighton Beach at 7:37 p.m.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Effective January 2, 1973, the daytime QJ was truncated to Broad Street as the J, and the M was extended beyond Broad Street during the day along the QJ's former route to Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue, via the Montague Street Tunnel and Brighton Line local tracks.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Also, changes were made to D and M service on the Brighton Line. Northbound weekday M train service originating at Kings Highway would begin at 5:46 a.m., while northbound service from Coney Island would begin at 6:34 a.m. From 5:40 to 6:34 a.m. northbound D trains would run local from Brighton Beach to Kings Highway, and then run express to Prospect Park. Late morning and early afternoon D trains would from then on run express from Brighton Beach to Kings Highway. The span of D express service to Brighton Beach was extended by 45 minutes to 9:05 p.m. from Prospect Park, and the span of M service from Broad Street to Coney Island was extended by 45 minutes over the span of QJ service to cover local stops.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Manhattan Bridge reconstruction and Brighton Line rehabilitation work (1982–2004)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=D_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Manhattan Bridge reconstruction and Brighton Line rehabilitation work (1982–2004)")]

For brief periods between 1982 and 1985, the Manhattan Bridge north tracks were out of service due to repairs, preventing D trains from operating along its normal route. In some instances, D trains were rerouted between Broadway–Lafayette Street and DeKalb Avenue via the [Nassau Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line") and the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"). In the northbound direction, D trains would operate via the Fourth Avenue Line from DeKalb Avenue to Court Street, via the Montague Street Tunnel, and then via the Nassau Street Line to Essex Street; at Essex Street, trains would change direction and utilize the Williamsburg Bridge connection of the Chrystie Street Connection and return to its normal route at Broadway–Lafayette Street. In the southbound direction, D trains would utilize the Williamsburg Bridge connection of the Chrystie Street Connection to Essex Street; at Essex Street, trains would change direction and operate via the Nassau Street Line, the Montague Street Tunnel, and the Fourth Avenue Line to DeKalb Avenue and return to its normal route. This reroute proved unsatisfactory because it delayed [J](/wiki/J_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "J (New York City Subway service)") and [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)") trains.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

D service was divided and ran in two sections when the north tracks of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") closed on April 26, 1986, due to construction, with regular service expected to resume on October 26, 1986. The northern section ran between Norwood–205th Street in the Bronx and 34th Street–Herald Square (the orange D) while the southern section ran express on the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") from [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)") to [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(BMT_Manhattan_Bridge_Line) "Canal Street (BMT Manhattan Bridge Line)"), then crossed the south tracks of the Manhattan Bridge into Brooklyn, and operated local along the Brighton Line to Stillwell Avenue (the yellow D). Service to Grand Street was replaced by the S shuttle, which ran via the Sixth Avenue local to 57th Street–Sixth Avenue.[[23]](#cite_note-:02-23)

At this time, the local tracks on the BMT Brighton Line also underwent reconstruction, necessitating the suspension of express service. As a substitute, the D and Q ran [skip-stop](/wiki/Skip-stop "Skip-stop") service between [Newkirk Avenue](/wiki/Newkirk_Plaza_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Newkirk Plaza (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Sheepshead Bay (BMT Brighton Line)") on weekdays. D trains served [Neck Road](/wiki/Neck_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Neck Road (BMT Brighton Line)"), [Avenue M](/wiki/Avenue_M_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue M (BMT Brighton Line)"), and [Avenue H](/wiki/Avenue_H_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue H (BMT Brighton Line)"); the Q served [Avenue U](/wiki/Avenue_U_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue U (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Avenue J](/wiki/Avenue_J_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue J (BMT Brighton Line)"), and both trains served [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Kings Highway (BMT Brighton Line)").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The first skip-stop train left Brighton Beach at about 6:30 a.m. while the last one left 57th Street–Seventh Avenue at about 7:30 p.m. On weekday evenings, between 8 p.m. and 1 a.m., D trains made all local stops, except Parkside Avenue and Beverley Road where service was only available in one direction. During late nights and weekends, D trains ran express between Prospect Park and Kings Highway depending on which tracks were being worked on.[[25]](#cite_note-25) By 1987, as reconstruction on the Brighton Line progressed, the weekday skip-stop pattern expanded to Prospect Park, with D trains serving [Beverley Road](/wiki/Beverley_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Beverley Road (BMT Brighton Line)") while Q trains served [Cortelyou Road](/wiki/Cortelyou_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Cortelyou Road (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Parkside Avenue](/wiki/Parkside_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Parkside Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)"), with [Church Avenue](/wiki/Church_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Church Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)") as a mutual station.[[26]](#cite_note-26) From November 23, 1987, to May 13, 1988, one AM rush hour D train was extended beyond its normal terminal at 57th Street/Seventh Avenue and terminated at [Astoria – Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria_%E2%80%93_Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria – Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On December 11, 1988, the north tracks of the Manhattan Bridge reopened and the two sections of the D joined running via Sixth Avenue Express. The D now ran as the full-time Brighton Local to Stillwell Avenue.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In January 1991, a reduction of service along the Concourse and Central Park West Corridors was proposed. Peak direction D service between [Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road_(IND_Concourse_Line) "Fordham Road (IND Concourse Line)") and [145th Street](/wiki/145th_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "145th Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") would be discontinued. In addition, the D would be the sole service along the Concourse Line due to the elimination (later changed to a reroute) of [C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)") service. This service change would have been implemented in October 1991, pending approval from the MTA board.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

From April 30 to November 12, 1995,[[31]](#cite_note-31) the Bridge's north tracks closed during middays and weekends and during these hours, D service was cut south of 34th Street-Herald Square. In its place, the Q ran local in Brooklyn to Stillwell Avenue.[[32]](#cite_note-32) On July 22, 2001, the north tracks were closed at all times and the southern (Broadway Line) tracks reopened. D service was again cut below 34th Street–Herald Square. In Brooklyn, D service was replaced by [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") local service.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 2004–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=D_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2004–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/1986-1988_Westinghouse_R68_2672_at_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Av.jpg/330px-1986-1988_Westinghouse_R68_2672_at_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Av.jpg)](/wiki/File:1986-1988_Westinghouse_R68_2672_at_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Av.jpg)

A [205th Street](/wiki/Norwood%E2%80%93205th_Street_station "Norwood–205th Street station")-bound D train of [R68s](/wiki/R68_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R68 (New York City Subway car)") awaits departure at [Coney Island-Stillwell Avenue station](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_station "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station")

On February 22, 2004, full service on the Manhattan Bridge was restored and D trains were extended via the north tracks of the bridge to Brooklyn, replacing the [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") as the [Fourth Avenue Express](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") (late nights local) and [West End Local](/wiki/BMT_West_End_Line "BMT West End Line") to Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The D was moved to the West End Line instead of returning to the Brighton Line, which it had run on since 1967, to provide 24-hour service to both the Concourse Line and West End Line and avoid running two separate (B and D) shortened services outside of weekdays. This eliminated the need to run late-night and weekend shuttles on the West End Line as was done prior to 2002.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

From May 24, 2004, to fall 2004, signal modernization on the [IND Concourse Line](/wiki/IND_Concourse_Line "IND Concourse Line") required the suspension of D express service in the Bronx.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

From September 18, 2021, until January 24, 2022,[[40]](#cite_note-MTA_2022-40) southbound D trains terminated at Bay 50th Street so work could be completed to protect Coney Island Yard from flooding.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The IND Concourse Line's express track was closed from July 2, 2022, to January 23, 2023, with D trains using the local tracks at all times.[[42]](#cite_note-Mass_Transit_2022-42)[[43]](#cite_note-Rivoli_2022-43) Effective December 15, 2024, four a.m. rush hour trains began entering service at 25th Avenue and four evening trains began terminating at Bay Parkway, instead of starting or ending at Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)
